data.title: Truist Financial: The Preferred Dividend Enjoys Excellent Coverage
data.description:
Introduction

Truist Financial (NYSE:TFC) is the holding company that owns Truist Bank, the oldest bank in North Carolina. The bank continued to grow in the past 150 years and is now a top-10 commercial bank in the US, servicing its customers with its network of almost 2,000 branches. As it had been a while since I had a look at its preferred shares, I wanted to see if the 6%-plus-yielding preferred stock is a good opportunity for fixed-income investors.

The bank’s results are good, and the preferred dividends are well covered

It goes without saying that one of the most important elements when looking at a preferred stock is to see how well those preferred dividends are covered.

Looking at the income statement provided by Truist Financial, we see the bank reported a total interest income of approximately $6.15B, a decrease of roughly $200M while the total amount of interest expenses decreased by just over $250M. The combination of both resulted in a net interest income increase of approximately $60 million, which represents just under 2%.

As the income statement below also shows, the bank reported a total non-interest income of $1.4B, while the total amount of non-interest expenses came in at just under $3B, including a $28 million restructuring expense.

Looking at the bottom line result, these elements add up to a pre-tax income of approximately $2B before taking the $488M in loan loss provisions into account. The reported net profit was roughly $1.24B, of which $1.18B was attributable to the common shareholders of the bank. This resulted in an EPS of $0.91, while the total earnings per share in the first semester came in at $1.80.

As the income statement above also shows, the bank needed less than 5% of its net profit to cover the preferred dividends in the second quarter. And looking at the more realistic H1 payout ratio (some preferred shares don’t have a quarterly payment schedule), we see that about 7% of the net income (which includes almost $1B in loan loss provisions and $66M in restructuring charges) was used to cover the preferred dividends.

The financial results also indicate that dividends on the common shares are quite well covered. The bank currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.52, which means that the payout ratio in the first half of this year was just under 60%. The dividend hasn't increased for a while now, so perhaps the strong financial results may also result in a dividend hike as the consensus estimates seem to be projecting an annualized dividend of $2.2 for FY 2027.

Another look at the Series R preferred shares

About 1.5 years ago, I focused on the bank’s Series R preferred shares, which are trading with (TFC.PR.R) as a ticker symbol. This series pays a fixed preferred dividend of 4.75% of its principal value of $25. Given the low cost of capital (4.75% for equity is pretty cheap), I doubt these securities will be called anytime soon (the first potential call date is in September). As such, I don’t think it's relevant to highlight the yield-to-call simply because a call appears to be unlikely (and even if they were called this year or next year, I’d be ecstatic to lock in a 28% capital gain).

Considering that the preferred shares are trading at a discount to the principal value of $25 per share, the current yield is approximately 6.1%. And given the strong result of the bank, I think the risk/reward ratio is pretty attractive.

Investment thesis

I currently have no position in any of the securities mentioned in this article, but as I'm looking to deploy more cash in fixed-income securities, I'm giving the preferred shares issued by Truist Financial another really good look. Additionally, I'm also not ruling out a long position in the common shares of the bank, given its interesting growth profile over the next few years, as it wants to open a hundred additional branches. These expansion plans also lend more credibility to the consensus estimates, which are pointing toward a 10% earnings growth in both 2026 and 2027.

If the consensus estimates are somewhat correct, the common shares of the bank are currently trading at just nine times the forward earnings for 2027, and that makes it an intriguing opportunity. I also noticed there are options available on Truist Financial, and I may consider writing some out-of-the-money put options.

data.title: Verb Technology: Better To Buy Toncoin Than VERB Stock
data.description:
Verb Technology Company Inc. (NASDAQ:VERB) has undergone a dramatic transformation in recent years. Originally positioned as a SaaS provider for interactive video-based sales tools, the company has pivoted into the fields of social commerce and digital asset management. The cause for the recent stock price movement is the decision to initiate a corporate treasury of Toncoin, the native cryptocurrency of The Open Network (TON) blockchain.

This has become much more common for companies to hold crypto on the balance sheet. This is even more than just buying crypto to store in the Treasury. The company recently announced that its treasury assets exceed $713 million of TON, which is more than half of its market cap. The company is also planning to rebrand as Ton Strategy Company. It is not merely adding crypto to the Treasury holdings, but more so that the company intends to put focus on crypto as a business.

My question becomes, is there any value in the underlying business, or is all the value in the holding of TON? If an investor is going to buy into the business just to hold TON, then why not go buy and hold TON directly? Is the underlying business going to hold back or increase the value of the Treasury holdings?

Company Overview

Verb operates at the intersection of livestream shopping, AI-powered video engagement, and most recently, blockchain-based treasury strategies. The company operates in three business units: MARKET.live, Go Fund Yourself (a TV show), and Vanity Prescribed/Good Girl Rx.

MARKET.live is an omnichannel livestream shopping marketplace designed to capitalize on the growth of influencer-led retail and video commerce. In April 2025, Verb expanded its reach through the acquisition of Lyvecom, an AI-driven, no-code platform that allows creators and brands to convert videos into shoppable content. Lyvecom also introduced automated virtual hosts and repurposing tools that significantly lower the barrier to entry for merchants and influencers seeking to scale video-driven e-commerce. This acquisition dovetails with MARKET.live, creating a more robust, integrated ecosystem.

Go Fund Yourself is a platform combines an interactive reality TV show that has been described as a combination of Shark Tank and Kickstarter with MARKET.live’s back-end capabilities allowing viewers to tap or scan onscreen icons and QR codes to facilitate an investment, in near real time, as they watch companies presenting before the Show’s panel of “Titans”. Presenting companies that sell consumer products are able to offer their products directly to viewers during the show in near real time through the same onscreen technology. (company filings)

Vanity Prescribed/Good Girl Rx is a telehealth initiative that operates in the rapid growth associated with the resale of the new weight-loss drugs. Vanity Prescribed leverages MARKET.live’s social commerce technology, which the Company intends to employ to disrupt the traditional healthcare model by utilizing social commerce capabilities to provide tailored healthcare solutions at affordable, fixed prices, without hidden fees, membership costs, or inflated pharmaceutical markups. (company filings)

The biggest move that the company has made was the launch of TON Strategy Co. in August, a new division backed by a heavily oversubscribed $558 million private placement. The initiative positions Verb as a treasury vehicle for Toncoin, the native cryptocurrency of the TON blockchain. We will discuss this in more detail later.

Financial Review

I first want to review the financials of Verb and see if there is any value in the company’s operations without any discussion on the treasure strategy. Verb’s financial trajectory has been volatile over the years with the business changes. In fiscal year 2024, revenue increased to $895,000, a more than 13x increase from the prior year. Although the amount of revenue it started with was nearly nothing. Revenues less than $1 million are not something to brag about. The momentum accelerated into 2025: first-quarter revenue reached $1.3 million, already surpassing the entire revenue base of 2024. The second quarter saw even higher revenues of $2.1 million. This growth in revenue has primarily come from Go Fund Yourself. The show launched in July 2024 and has started to make significant contributions to revenue. Market.Live has also seen its revenues increase. The table below outlines the revenues for each business unit and the company as a whole.

Revenue from Go Fund Yourself has grown significantly since launch and has far surpassed MARKET.Live in revenue. My question is: how much growth can the company have from one show, and how sustainable is that growth? Yes, it is showing really good growth and still has a lot of room to run. Revenues of $1.4 million are not that large, and I would expect further growth there. The show could lose popularity, and revenues could decrease at any time. I don’t know if revenues from 1 TV show are a sustainable business. In the long term, I just don’t see this as a sustainable source of growth. The company will likely have to create new shows in the future and hope they stick with the audience.

MARKET.Live is also seeing good growth. Revenues increased 22% quarter over quarter. The only problem is that it is coming off a small revenue base. Those growth rates will have to continue for a while before they are able to push revenues high enough to break even. The company is currently losing more money than it is generating in revenue. That means it needs large growth for years to come before it has a chance to get to profitability.

The company ended Q2 with over $10 million in cash or equivalents on the balance sheet. The company burned through $3.35 million during the quarter. I would expect that rate to decrease as revenues continue to grow. That being said, the company only has 3 quarters of a runway at the current burn rate.

TON Strategy

The main cause for the recent stock spike is due to the news that Verb raised $558 million to purchase TON as its primary Treasury reserve asset. This news caused the stock price to jump from $9.51 a share to over $20 in a trading day.

The company noted the following its TON Strategy:

“The company's strategy targets the accumulation of over 5% of $TON’s circulating supply, establishing the Company as a significant participant in maintaining and securing the network infrastructure. In addition, the Company intends to steadily increase $TON held per share through reinvestment of cash flows, staking rewards, and disciplined capital markets activity… The strategy is expected to generate sustainable staking rewards to initiate, manage, and grow the company's $TON exposure in a cash flow-positive manner. The Company intends to rebrand as TON Strategy Co. (TSC) to reflect its new direction.”

So what are TON and Toncoin? From Wikipedia:

“TON, also known as The Open Network (previously Telegram Open Network), is a decentralized layer-1 blockchain. TON was originally developed by Nikolai Durov, who is also known for his role in creating the messaging platform Telegram.

Telegram had planned to use TON to launch its own cryptocurrency (Gram), but was forced to abandon the project in 2020 following an injunction by US regulators. The network was then renamed, and independent developers have created their own cryptocurrencies and decentralized applications (dApps) using TON. Toncoin, the principal token of The Open Network, is deeply integrated into the Telegram messaging app, used for paying rewards to creators and developers, buying Telegram ads, hosting giveaways, or purchasing services such as Telegram Premium.”

Telegram has nearly one billion monthly active users. It is easy to see why TON represents one of the fastest-growing blockchain ecosystems. Verb aims to leverage this integration by building one of the largest publicly traded Toncoin treasuries in existence.

Outlook and Growth Catalysts

Verb’s growth prospects are tied to livestream commerce. I expect the global livestream shopping market to continue to grow, driven by social media integration and influencer-led retail. MARKET.live and Lyvecom provide Verb with direct exposure to this trend. Verb is a very small player in this market with revenues of $688 thousand during the quarter. I do not know if it will ever be able to establish itself as a dominant player in the market. Go Fund Yourself is also in the same vein as livestream commerce. The company is seeing strength in the show, but I see limitations on the long-term growth potential.

The TON Strategy initiative adds a new growth driver. Verb effectively holds an asset-backed growth model tied to an expanding blockchain ecosystem. This provides shareholders with both operational upside and exposure to crypto market appreciation. If TON adoption within the Telegram ecosystem accelerates as expected, Verb could benefit from both Treasury appreciation and strategic positioning as a publicly traded TON proxy.

Price and Value

At roughly $20 per share, Verb’s market capitalization hovers around $1.25 billion. The company announced that it had $780 million in the Treasury, with $713 million being in TON. If we take that off the market cap, we are left with a market cap of nearly half a billion. I have a hard time justifying that valuation with an estimated annual revenue of $5.5 million and an operating loss for the year. The company would have to grow revenues at a very high rate for many years to grow into that valuation.

Much of the value is coming from the potential growth of their treasury strategy and the growth of TON. If an investor thinks that TON will appreciate its price, they would be better off buying it directly and not being tied to the loss-making entity.

Risks and Headwinds

There are a lot of risks for the company. Execution risk looms large. MARKET.live has shown promising growth, but revenues remain in the low single millions. Scaling this to a level that can justify its valuation and sustain long-term profitability will require significant adoption by merchants and influencers, and the competitive landscape is crowded with well-funded rivals such as TikTok Shop and Amazon Live.

The TON Strategy Co. pivot introduces heavy reliance on cryptocurrency markets. While Toncoin has shown rapid adoption, it remains volatile and subject to both speculative cycles and regulatory uncertainty.

Finally, shareholder dilution remains a risk. While the $558 million financing provides ample capital, further capital raises, acquisitions, or share issuances could impact existing shareholder value.

Investment Verdict

I don’t see the operating business being near the value assigned to it. The company is showing strong growth rates, mostly related to its Go Fund Yourself business unit. I think there is continued room for growth within the space. I also think of the MARKET.Live operates in a growing market. The company is just far away from any real profits and value, with revenues expected at $5.5 million for the year.

If an investor wants exposure to TON, then I would suggest that the investor go and purchase the crypto directly rather than gain exposure through a loss-making entity. The company will have to supplement its loss-making operations with any staking profits from TON. Better to buy the crypto and reinvest any staking rewards. It is not available on all the large exchanges due to its investigation by the SEC, but it is available for purchase on some major exchanges (Kraken).

I am not endorsing the purchase of TON or doing an evaluation on the crypto. Obviously, the value of Verb is tied to the crypto at this point, but if you think the future of Verb is bright because of TON, then I think you would be better served purchasing the crypto directly. For this reason, I do not recommend purchasing the stock. The stock very well may see a price increase due to the appreciation of TON, but that return would be better if you owned the coin directly.

data.title: NVIDIA, Dell Set To Report Earnings As Investors Focus On Core PCE Price Index
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street jumped sharply on Friday, with the Dow hitting a fresh all-time high as investors welcomed Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's remarks at Jackson Hole. At Jackson Hole, Powell remarked that a shifting balance of risks "may warrant adjusting our policy stance," signaling a potential willingness to lower the central bank's policy rate if conditions justify it. "Risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside - a challenging situation," Powell added. As a result, the odds of a rate cut in September rose as the chance of a September cut was at ~71% before the speech and rose to ~93% after it.

With market sentiment on edge, investors must keep an eye on economic reports due to be released next week, such as Building Permits and Chicago Fed National Activity on Monday, Durable Goods Orders, and House Price Index on Tuesday, with reports such as MBA Mortgage Applications, Crude Oil Imports, and Gasoline Production on Wednesday. Thursday will include reports on Core PCE Prices, GDP, and Pending Home Sales, with the Core PCE Price Index, Chicago PMI, Personal Income, and Personal Spending set to be released on Friday.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Ample Reserves, Dividends, Growth, And Repurchase Program, Despite Oil Price Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ) a buy rating. I am of the opinion that the company will continue to create value for investors. This year, CNQ expects to increase its production by 7.90%. In addition, since at least 2016, its production has consistently grown year-over-year. CNQ has repurchased its common shares. From March of this year to March 2026, it expects to buy back up to 10% of its outstanding common shares. Furthermore, the company pays dividends. From 2020 to the present, it has paid CA$12.31 per share in dividends. To grant context, at the end of 2023, CNQ's stock price was CA$21.00 per share.

CNQ still has the potential to perform well. As of 2024, it had a proved reserve life index of 33 years. In addition, it has reported positive earnings in seven of the last eight years, indicating that it is a profitable firm.

However, lower oil prices could undermine the company's financial performance. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects that Brent crude oil will average $51 per barrel in 2026. In addition, the high debt level is another risk factor. The company had a 51.57% liabilities-to-assets ratio in Q2 2025, a higher ratio than those reported by other Canadian companies. Finally, Canadian green policies could be a hurdle to CNQ's production. Nonetheless, when pondering all positive and negative aspects of CNQ, I am still of the opinion that the company is an excellent investment opportunity.

Motivation

In April of this year, I wrote my first article on CNQ. Since that day, the company's stock price has increased by only 0.47%. Despite the low return, I still believe that CNQ represents an interesting investment opportunity. The critical point is to be patient and wait until the investment generates results.

Companies have different ways to create value for shareholders. The first one is to reinvest its resources to expand its operations. With this strategy, companies will increase their revenues, generating higher earnings. The second option is to pay dividends to its investors. Another alternative is to implement a repurchase program, reducing its common shares. In the following three sections, I will discuss how CNQ has created value for its investors through these three methods.

Production

CNQ has expanded its production. In 2017, its oil production was 962.26 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day (Mboe/d), increasing to 1,420.36 Mboe/d in Q2 2025, for a 47.61% growth, as depicted in the following chart. The company has increased its production year-over-year. Even in 2020, a year in which the oil market was affected by COVID-19, CNQ expanded its oil and gas output.

For this year, the company expects to increase its production to 1,532.5 Mboe/d, the midpoint of its guidance, representing a 7.90% gain on a yearly basis. To accomplish this goal, it will invest CA$6,050 million in capital expenditures (CapEx). Furthermore, in July of this year, the company acquired assets in Montney, Canada, that were producing 32 Mboe/d. This acquisition will boost CNQ's production even further.

As is to be expected, the revenues of CNQ have increased due to the higher volume of oil and gas sold by the company. Its revenues were CA$21,027 million in 2018, reaching CA$35,656 million in 2024. Meanwhile, the net income grew to CA$6,106 million in 2024, for a 135.66% growth compared to 2018. To provide context, the annual average of the WTI oil price only increased by 17.48% in the same period.

The higher volume of production of CNQ has caused a positive effect on its stock price. In fact, the company's stock price has increased by 197.96% over a five-year period, as depicted in the following chart. As such, investors have benefited as a result of higher production levels.

Common Shares

The company has consistently repurchased its common shares. From 2018 to Q2 2025, its weighted average common shares outstanding have declined by 12.13%, as depicted in the following chart. At first glance, this might not seem to be a significant reduction. However, CNQ's strategy of buying back its common shares has created value for investors.

In March of this year, the company approved a repurchase program. It expects to buy up to 10% of its common shares between March 13, 2025, and March 12, 2026. This decision will have two positive effects for shareholders. The first is that investors' CNQ common shares could increase by up to 10%. In addition, it will reduce the supply of CNQ's common shares, pushing the company's stock price up.

Dividends

Another way to create value for investors is through dividends. From 2020 to now, CNQ has paid CA$12.31 per share in dividends. To provide context, at the end of 2019, CNQ's stock price was CA$16.18. As such, those investors who bought shares in CNQ that day and held the position until the present would have received an equivalent of 76.10% of the purchase price in dividends.

Proved Reserves

In 2024, CNQ has 15.2 billion boe of proved reserves. If the company maintains its oil and gas production at the level of 2024, its proved reserves will last for 33 years. Consequently, the company can extract oil for years without acquiring new lands. CNQ's proved reserves include oil and gas. For instance, the U.S. crude oil and lease condensate proved reserves were 46.4 billion barrels in 2023.

To add another comparison, Warren Buffett, the famous investor, indicated that the ample fossil reserves of Occidental Petroleum (OXY) were one of the reasons that led Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) to invest in that oil company. As of 2024, Occidental Petroleum had 4.6 billion boe of proved reserves and a proved reserve life index of 8.61 years, considerably lower than that of CNQ. As such, investments in CNQ are even more profitable.

Profit Margin

CNQ has a high profit margin, which is a competitive advantage. It has achieved positive earnings in seven of the last eight years, as illustrated in the following chart. So far, in the second quarter of this year, it reported a 28.27% net income margin. It only reported a net loss of 2.58% in 2020, which is not a significant loss. That year, COVID-19 negatively impacted the global oil demand. In fact, the WTI oil price averaged $39.16 per barrel. Therefore, even in a very adverse year, the company was not severely impacted.

Low Declining Assets

CNQ has low declining assets. This means that the company can extract oil from its assets for years without those assets depleting. In fact, the current CNQ's production decline rate is 11%. This implies that the company does not have to invest significant resources in capital expenditure to keep up its production. On the other hand, fracking companies require substantial investments in capex to maintain their production levels.

To illustrate the advantage that CNQ has compared to oil and gas fracking companies, the following table shows that CNQ's depreciation and amortization (D&A) expenses only represent 18.15% of the company's revenues. I obtained this ratio by subtracting the company's EBITDA margin from its EBIT margin. Meanwhile, OXY, Diamondback Energy (FANG), Permian Resources (PR), Matador Resources (MTDR), and EOG Resources (EOG) have higher D&A-to-revenues ratios than CNQ. Excluding CNQ, the other firms are oil fracking companies.

For instance, FANG has a 31.77% D&A-to-revenue ratio. This ratio indicates that the company has to invest significant resources to keep up its production. If FANG does not invest substantial resources in capex, its production will experience a significant decline in one year. In contrast, CNQ could reduce its capex, and its production will not be severely affected.

Trans Mountain Pipeline

In May 2024, the expansion of the Trans Mountain Pipeline began its commercial operations. This pipeline transports oil from Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, to the Pacific coast of that country. With the expansion, the pipeline increased its daily capacity from 300,000 barrels of oil per day to 890,000 barrels of oil per day. As such, Canadian oil has more access to the international market, improving the price paid per barrel of oil. Without this infrastructure, CNQ would have to sell its oil production at a significant discount compared to the WTI.

In addition, Canada has increased its oil exports to the U.S. Gulf Coast. For instance, between 2013 and 2023, Canadian oil exports to the U.S. Gulf Coast increased by 505%. This growth has fueled demand for Canadian oil.

The expansion of the Trans Mountain Pipeline and higher exports of Canadian oil to the U.S. Gulf Coast have contributed to a decline in the WTI-to-WCS ratio (WCS is the Canadian oil price benchmark). In fact, in the first six months of this year, this ratio averaged 1.22, declining to values not seen since 2011, as depicted in the following chart. In June of this year, the ratio decreased to 1.17. A lower ratio is better for Canadian oil producers, since these firms will sell their production at a price closer to the WTI oil price. Ultimately, CNQ's revenues and earnings will be positively affected.

Debt Risk

I think that debt is a negative aspect of CNQ. In Q2 2025, the company had a 51.57% liabilities-to-assets ratio, as depicted in the following chart. This ratio indicates that more than half of the company is composed of debt. If energy prices decline, the company could experience financial difficulties in paying its obligations.

This section will be incomplete without including other Canadian oil and gas companies in the analysis. The following table shows the debt-to-equity and current ratios of CNQ, Cenovus Energy (CVE), Suncor (SU), Imperial Oil Limited (IMO), Baytex Energy (BTE), MEG Energy (OTCPK:MEGEF), Obsidian Energy (OBE), Athabasca Oil (OTCPK:ATHOF), and Tourmaline Oil (OTCPK:TRMLF). CNQ has the second-highest debt-to-equity ratio, a negative aspect. In addition, it has the third-lowest current ratio. These figures indicate that CNQ relies on debt to fund its operations.

Carbon Capture

Several hurdles affect the oil production in Canada. The Canadian green policy is one of them. There is a proposal to limit the oil and gas greenhouse gas emissions in Canada. This proposal aims to cap oil and gas emissions for the first compliance period, 2030-2032, at 27% below the emissions that will be reported in 2026.

CNQ has implemented initiatives to reduce its gas emissions. The company has a facility that can capture 2.7 million tonnes of CO2 per year. This year, it plans to invest CA$90 million in carbon capture. However, this facility is insufficient to reduce the company's oil and gas emissions significantly.

For instance, in 2023, the company said that it expects to reduce its gas emissions by 22 megatonnes, or 22 million tonnes, per year by 2030. Consequently, CNQ has achieved only 12.27% of its plan capture program. If the Canadian government restricts the oil and gas greenhouse emissions, CNQ will have to invest significant resources to reduce its emissions.

Price Risk

Oil prices have declined by 30.51% over the last three years. The WTI oil price trades at $62.52 per barrel. For 2026, the EIA projects that Brent crude oil will average $51 per barrel. This crude oil generally trades at a higher price than the WTI oil price. If oil prices continue to decline, CNQ revenues and net income will be negatively impacted.

Peer Analysis

This section conducts a peer analysis among some of the most relevant oil and gas producers in Canada. To implement the comparison, I selected the following companies: CNQ, CVE, SU,IMO, BTE, MEGEF, OBE, ATHOF, and TRMLF. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, the CNQ's stock price declined by 14.33%, a further drop than the median value. In addition, over a five-year period, its stock price increased by 282.79%, lower than the median value. Both figures indicate that CNQ has underperformed among its peers.

However, the company is expected to pay a dividend yield of 5.71%, the second-highest value among its peers. In addition, it paid, on average, a dividend yield of 4.66% in the last four years, higher than the median ratio, which is a positive aspect.

Moving to the valuation metrics, CNQ has higher EV/EBITDA, price-to-book, and price-to-cash flow ratios than the median of these ratios, indicating that the company is overvalued compared to its peers.

On the other hand, the company's revenues are expected to decline by 1.53% this year. In addition, over a five-year period, the revenues increased at a compound annual growth rate of 14.35%. Both ratios are lower than their respective medians. Therefore, CNQ has a worse revenue performance compared to the median ratios.

Finally, the profitability ratios indicate that CNQ is more profitable than the median ratios in the peer group. CNQ has a net income margin of 21.90%, considerably higher than the median ratio. Further, it has the highest unleveraged FCF margin with 17.48%.

Overall, the company has seven ratios with better results than their respective medians. It also has seven ratios with worse results than their respective medians. However, it has better results in key metrics, such as profit margins. In addition, its revenues have grown over the last five years.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I think that CNQ represents an excellent investment opportunity. The key aspect is the long term. The company's stock price could decline in the short term. However, in the long term, CNQ's stock price will likely increase.

To begin with, the company repurchases its common shares. Consequently, investors will have more common shares over time. Furthermore, its oil and natural gas production grew by 47.61% from 2017 to Q2 2025. Meanwhile, for this year, it plans to expand its production by 7.90% compared to 2024. CNQ achieved these results thanks to its profit margin and its ample oil reserves.

THE EIA projects lower oil prices for 2026, a risk factor for CNQ. In addition, in Q2 2025, the company had a 51.57% liabilities-to-assets ratio, indicating that more than half of the company's assets are composed of debt. Furthermore, Canadian green policies could limit CNQ's oil production. Ultimately, when taking all of these aspects into consideration, I still consider CNQ to be an excellent investment opportunity.

data.title: Where Apartment Rents May Accelerate Soonest
data.description:
By Mike Sobolik, Investment Strategist, Direct Real Estate, North America

Introduction

Market conditions have been shifting in the U.S. apartment sector, one of the most highly represented property types in institutional real estate.1 U.S. apartment construction starts for the year ending 2Q 2025 fell to their lowest level since 2012, while tenant demand reached a record high.2 This imbalance suggests that many apartment markets could see vacancies tighten, potentially supporting significant rent growth over the next one to two years.

Rent growth has long been a key driver of apartment value growth. Over the past 20 years, the correlation between annual effective rent growth3 and annual property appreciation has been very strong at 0.80.4

The importance of rent growth becomes more pronounced when compared at the submarket level. From 1Q-2000 to 2Q-2025, the average five-year rent growth spread between top- and bottom-quartile submarkets was 4.8 percentage points (lowest: 3.2 points). This wide gap highlights the opportunity to outperform by selecting submarkets with stronger rent growth potential.

We have developed a framework to identify which markets/submarkets we believe will be best positioned to drive outsized rent growth the soonest. This paper walks through a simplified version of this framework.

Methodology for projecting high rent growth submarkets

  • We focused on U.S. metropolitan areas with at least 100,000 apartment units.5 (53 metro areas; 723 submarkets).
  • We then identified metro-level factors most strongly correlated with effective rent growth.
  • Occupancy rates, the factor most highly correlated with metro rent growth, was projected for submarkets based on current occupancy/vacancy, units currently under construction, and trailing one-year demand levels.
  • We used those projections to find submarkets most likely in the coming year to surpass 96% occupancy, the point at which rent growth tends to speed up. (494 of 723 met this criterion)
  • These were narrowed further to submarkets in metros where 75%+ of inventory is projected to exceed 96% critical occupancy (260 submarkets).
  • Finally, these 260 submarkets were differentiated by metro job growth and metro cap rates6 to assess investment tradeoffs.

Key drivers of rent growth

Among the 53 metros analyzed, occupancy rates and job growth were most consistently correlated with effective rent growth7:

  • Occupancy rate correlation average: 0.72 (standard deviation8: 0.09)
  • Job growth correlation average: 0.61 (standard deviation: 0.11)

Other tested factors—demand growth, supply growth, construction starts, units under construction, and Baa bond rates9—showed lower average correlations and less consistent correlations across metros (i.e., higher standard deviations).

Occupancy data is available at the submarket level, while job growth is only available at the metro level. Therefore, occupancy was used first to filter submarkets, with job growth applied later.

Critical occupancy level

Rent growth aggregated to the national level tends to accelerate when national average occupancy exceeds 96%. Although this threshold may not apply uniformly across submarkets, it serves as a useful guide for filtering submarkets.

Nationally, rent growth tends to increase when occupancy exceeds 96%

U.S. apartment occupancy and rent growth (%)

1Q-2000 to 2Q-2025

Submarket projections

Next, we applied the 96% occupancy threshold to current conditions in all 723 submarkets:

  • Total availability was estimated by combining 2Q 2025 vacancies with units under construction.
  • We then compared total availability to two demand scenarios:
    • Demand continues at the pace of the past year (which was at or near historical highs in many areas due to higher mortgage rates weakening affordability).
    • Demand reverts to the trailing 10-year average.

Under the first scenario, 494 submarkets (68%) are projected to reach or exceed 96% occupancy by mid-2026. If demand reverts to the 10-year average, this number drops significantly. However, we believe, strained homeownership affordability supports the likelihood of continued strong apartment demand.

Critical mass metros

The 494 submarkets projected to reach 96% occupancy in the next year are spread across all 53 metros. Some metros have many such submarkets; others have only one.

We believe submarkets surrounded by several high-occupancy, low-supply areas are more likely to sustain stronger rent growth. Narrowing our focus to metros where 75%+ of inventory are in submarkets projected to reach critical occupancy in the next year (what we’ll call “critical mass metros”) results in 260 submarkets in 23 metros.

Submarkets on pace to reach critical occupancy within critical mass metros

260 out of 723 submarkets

Investment tradeoffs

The goal of this analysis goes beyond identifying submarkets with the greatest likelihood of significant rent growth. It’s to find submarkets with the best prospects for total return performance potential. For that reason, we analyzed the 260 submarkets using:

  1. Metro job growth: Strongly correlated with metro effective rent growth.
  2. Cap rates: Reflects the price investors pay for property income growth.

Comparing these 260 submarkets with metro job growth in 2024 and cap rates reported in 2Q 2025 provides perspective about potential investment tradeoffs:

  • High job growth, high cap rate submarkets (15%, or 39 submarkets) - Best positioned for stronger rent growth at attractive pricing.
  • Low job growth, high cap rate submarkets (48%, or 125 submarkets) - This category accounts for nearly half of the 260 submarkets. Must assess whether higher cap rates offset the risks of slower demand growth.
  • High job growth, low cap rate submarkets (5%, or 12 submarkets) - Investors are paying a premium for expected growth. This requires high confidence in the strength and sustainability of job growth.
  • Low job growth and low cap rate submarkets (6%, or 15 submarkets) - Risk of overpaying in slower job growth metros despite high occupancy.

This simple framework is useful but has limitations.

  • 2024 job growth may not reflect future trends. A well-rounded framework would examine more than one reasonable scenario for future job growth.
  • Metro-level cap rates can differ from property-level rates.
  • Comparative submarket strength can vary widely due to differences in performance drivers, such as the growth posture of local employers; current and changing local housing regulations; gaps between the local cost of homeownership versus renting; the degree of local availability of housing alternatives. None of these factors are explicitly part of this analysis but should be factored when comparing potential submarket strength.

Still, this framework provides a useful starting point for selecting submarkets.

Submarkets on pace to reach critical occupancy within critical mass metros

One-year critical occupancy submarkets by relative metro cap rate and metro job growth

260 out of 723 submarkets

Regional differences

Going back to the 494 submarkets expected to reach or exceed 96% occupancy within the next year, clear regional patterns emerge:

  • Northeast: Most submarkets already exceed the 96% critical occupancy rate.
  • Midwest and West: Increasing number of critical occupancy submarkets.
  • South: Usually posts the strongest job growth yet also contains the largest supply pipeline at present; slower path to critical occupancy.

Northeast and Midwest have higher percentages of critical occupancy submarkets

Percent of submarkets per region exceeding or approaching critical occupancy rate of 96%

Conclusion: Use data to improve performance probability

Understanding key drivers — occupancy rates, job growth, and cap rates—can improve investment decisions about apartment submarket locations. Although no framework guarantees perfect outcomes, this kind of approach may improve the probability of achieving better relative performance potential.

Defined Terms and Notes:

  1. Source: NCREIF Property Index (NPI) as of July 2025. NCREIF Property Index (NPI) is published by the National Council of Real Estate Investment Fiduciaries (NCREIF). NPI is a quarterly, unleveraged composite total return for private commercial real estate properties held for investment purposes only. All properties in the NPI have been acquired, at least in part, on behalf of tax-exempt institutional investors and held in a fiduciary environment. In 2Q-2025, the apartment sector represented 29.7% of total market value in the NPI. There may be material differences in the investment goals, liquidity needs, and investment horizons of individual and institutional investors. Investors should consult with a financial professional regarding their own situation and risk tolerance before making any investment decisions.
  2. Source: RealPage as of July 2025. Annual U.S. apartment construction starts at the end of 2Q-2025 totaled 215,195 units, the lowest level since 1Q-2012 at 196,156 units. Annual tenant demand for the year ending 2Q-2025 totaled 794,160 units, the highest level since RealPage began to keep records in 2000.
  3. Effective rent growth is the percent change in average effective rent per unit between two time periods. Effective rent represents the actual rent level at which units on average are rented after offerings of free rent and other concessions are deducted from the advertised rent level (typically referred to as “asking rent”).
  4. Source: RealPage and NCREIF as of July 2025. Correlations between year-over year-growth of effective rents and indexed property values from the NCREIF Property Index were calculated from 3Q-2005 to 2Q-2025. Correlation indicates the degree to which two investments have historically moved in the same direction and magnitude.
  5. Apartment data used in this report is sourced from Real Page as of July 2025.
  6. Capitalization rates (cap rates) is the quotient of a property’s net operating income divided by the property’s estimate value.
  7. Data for this analysis reflect conditions between the periods of 1Q-2000 to 2Q-2025.
  8. Standard Deviation is a statistic that measures the dispersion of a dataset relative to its mean.
  9. A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments or other debts. Ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest); ratings are subject to change without notice. NR indicates the debtor was not rated and should not be interpreted as indicating low quality. For more information on rating methodologies, please visit the following NRSRO websites: www.standardandpoors.com and select 'Understanding Credit Ratings' under Rating Resources 'About Ratings' on the homepage.; https://ratings.moodys.io/ratings and select 'Understanding Ratings' on the homepage.; www.fitchratings.com and select 'Ratings Definitions Criteria' under 'Resources' on the homepage. Then select 'Rating Definitions' under 'Resources' on the 'Contents' menu.

Investment risks

The value of investments and any income will fluctuate (this may partly be the result of exchange rate fluctuations) and investors may not get back the full amount invested. Property and land can be difficult to sell, so investors may not be able to sell such investments when they want to. The value of property is generally a matter of an independent valuer’s opinion and may not be realised.

Generally, real estate assets are illiquid in nature. Although certain kinds of investments are expected to generate current income, the return of capital and the realization of gains, if any, from an investment will often occur upon the partial or complete disposition of such investment.

Investing in real estate typically involves a moderate to high degree of risk. The possibility of partial or total loss of capital will exist. Investing in commercial real estate assets involves certain risks, including but not limited to: tenants' inability to pay rent; increases in interest rates and lack of availability of financing; tenant turnover and vacancies; and changes in supply of or demand for similar property types in a given market.

Important information

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions if they are uncertain whether an investment is suitable for them.

Data as of August 2025, unless otherwise stated.

Where individuals or the business have expressed opinions, they are based on current market conditions, they may differ from those of other investment professionals and are subject to change without notice.

Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions, there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

For U.S. audiences:

Invesco Distributors, Inc. is the U.S. distributor for Invesco’s retail products and private placement, and is an indirect, wholly owned subsidiary of Invesco Ltd.

For Canadian audiences:

Commissions, trailing commissions, management fees and expenses may all be associated with mutual fund investments. Mutual funds are not guaranteed, their values change frequently and past performance may not be repeated. Please read the simplified prospectus before investing. Copies are available from your advisor or from Invesco Canada Ltd.

Most references are U.S. centric and may not apply to Canada. All data is USD, unless otherwise stated.

This document is issued in Canada by Invesco Canada Ltd. 16 York Street, Suite 1200, Toronto, Ontario, M5J 0E6. Invesco® and all associated trademarks are trademarks of Invesco Holding Company Limited, used under license.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

Mid-cap value stocks, after selling off sharply in early April following Washington’s announcement of an unexpectedly aggressive trade policy, recovered to close the quarter firmly in positive territory. Investor sentiment steadily improved thanks to the combination of healthy corporate earnings, falling inflation, and the growing perception that the worst of the tariffs wouldn’t come to pass, fueling an impressive rebound that helped the broad-based indexes finish June at all-time highs. Growth stocks and higher beta, lower-quality companies generally led the way in the recovery, while the value style underperformed.

Contributors and detractors

Stock selection in the information technology and financials sectors contributed to relative performance. In the former, the software company Oracle Corp. was the top contributor in the sector and the fund as a whole. The company exceeded revenue and earnings expectations and raised guidance as its cloud infrastructure business continued to benefit from increased AI-related business opportunities. In financials, the banking giant JP Morgan Chase & Co.—which benefited from an improving earnings outlook, favorable interest rate trends, and the prospect of reduced regulation—led the way. A position in American Express Co. and a zero weighting in Berkshire Hathaway, Inc. were further contributors of note.

Healthcare stocks UnitedHealth Group, Inc. and AbbVie, Inc. were among the largest detractors. UnitedHealth recently replaced its CEO and rescinded its guidance as its Medicare billing practices were allegedly being investigated by the U.S. Department of Justice. We trimmed the fund’s weighting in the stock during the quarter, but we maintained a position based in part on its attractive valuation and the strong track record of the new CEO. AbbVie was hurt by weaker- than-expected revenues for cosmetic drugs as consumers tightened their spending habits. Holdings in a number of energy stocks also weighed on performance. The sector came under pressure from weakness in oil prices and investors’ preference for higher-growth companies.

Portfolio changes

The fund was overweight in sectors in which we found the largest number of companies with the combination of robust fundamentals, attractive valuations, and improving business momentum. These included information technology, industrials, and financials, as well as energy, materials, and utilities.

The fund also holds a high representation of distribution companies across the value chain through positions in a variety of stocks in the consumer staples, consumer discretionary, mining, and industrials sectors. We think distribution businesses can implement nominal price increases in response to an inflationary environment, which should have a positive impact on earnings for companies that can maintain their profit margins.

Although the bounce off of the April lows was certainly good news for investors, we expect volatility will remain elevated in the coming months. Tariff news, conflicts overseas, and uncertainty regarding the U.S. Federal Reserve’s next move could all affect investor sentiment in the second half of the year. We believe this unstable backdrop should continue to provide ample opportunities for bottom-up stock selection.

data.title: Understanding The History Of This 24% Yield: ECC
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

"If you stand for nothing, Burr, what’ll you fall for?"

– Alexander Hamilton, from Hamilton

One of the biggest misunderstandings and misconceptions in modern society is that one can simply adapt one's values to align with what is popular at the time. The idea that you can ride the waves of public opinion successfully and find success or renown through it is a dangerous game at best. The crowd is a fickle beast and is just as ready to embrace you as it is to disown you and destroy you on a moment's notice.

Those who find the most success in the public field accept both the popularity and the notoriety that come with their strong positions and opinions on various subject matters. It's those who have no set opinions or positions that are often seen as hollow or foolish. Aaron Burr was famously known for not having strong set opinions on many different matters, and Alexander Hamilton ended up not supporting him when he ran for president because he'd rather support Jefferson, who he actively disagreed with, because he stood for something than someone who he couldn't understand what they stood for because they were just looking to be popular.

When it comes to the market, different sectors or companies will be favored or disliked by the market at various times, and this measurement is often referred to as sentiment. Market sentiment refers to the collective momentum or opinion held by the public regarding various sectors and companies. It will drive their share price in the short term. Market sentiment can have a significant impact in the short term, as it prevails and can result in double-digit or even triple-digit returns via price changes.

When the meme stock craze occurred, GameStop (GME) skyrocketed, delivering triple-digit returns for shareholders who had invested before the phenomenon began. This wasn't driven by the fundamental health of GameStop. No, it was driven by market sentiment.

Being an income investor and a frequent writer and researcher, my drive isn't to be the most popular author or investor in the market, but to stand behind what I believe with the evidence provided by the fundamentals and then use market sentiment to my benefit to massively boost the income from my holdings that I feel are oversold because of market sentiment.

Today, I want to examine a fund that has been heavily beaten down by market sentiment and see what's happening beneath the surface.

Let's dive in!

Sentiment Shifts and Twists

Eagle Point Credit Company (NYSE:ECC), yielding 24.4, is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that invests in CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations), where it primarily focuses on investing at the "equity" level.

ECC has seen a declining share price along with peer Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC) in recent months.

Note: We regularly cover ECC. This article serves as an educational update on this often misunderstood fund.

When these companies disclosed that their NAV (Net Asset Value) declined a lot in April, the market panicked. From December 31st to April 30th, ECC's NAV declined from $8.38 to $6.76/share—a 19% decline. The market underestimated this decline, with ECC's share price declining only 12.5% during the period.

When ECC was down 20% in early April, it's likely that its NAV was down substantially more than that. At the end of April, the recovery was already underway. ECC might only report NAV one day a month, but it changes every day, whether you see it or not.

Since April 30th, ECC's NAV has been on the road to recovery: Source

What did ECC's price do? It continued downward. In fact, the steepest decline occurred in July, with ECC's price falling by 12%, and we now know that during the same period ECC's NAV increased 2.5%:

The moral of the story is that share price movements are not the same as NAV movements, although we often see commentators conflate the two.

ECC's NAV was $7.45 on June 30th, 2020. Within the range that ECC projects, its NAV was $7.45 on July 30th, 2025. We see commentators declaring ECC's NAV decline since 2021 as PROOF! That somehow ECC has been overpaying its distribution.

But if ECC's NAV was $7.45 in June 2020 and is $7.44-$7.54 today, where is the decay? Where is the overpayment? It appears that the $9.41 in distributions that ECC has paid out since June 2020 was almost exactly covered by cash flow and/or gains. They were worth $7.45 in June 2020; they paid out $9.41 in distributions, and today the NAV is $7.44 or higher. So maybe they lost a penny.

Of course, if you look at the picture from 2021, it is very different.

ECC had a NAV of $14.28 in October 2021, and someone who bought then has collected $8.41 in distributions. As a result, a shareholder "lost" $6.84 in NAV and gained $8.41 in distributions, for a net gain of just $1.57—an 11% return on the NAV of $14.28 over 4 years. That isn't an impressive-sounding number because it isn't a great return over that period.

These are the games that people can play to manipulate facts to fit their narrative. Whether you bought in June 2020 or June 2021 made a huge difference in whether the NAV of your shares is about the same or the NAV of your shares has declined a lot.

What is NAV? It is the current liquidation value of ECC's holdings. If ECC were to sell everything, the NAV is how much money would theoretically be left. I say "theoretically" because CLO equity is not publicly traded, it is not a liquid asset, and if ECC had a fire sale, we don't think the number they get is going to be NAV; it would probably be a good bit less. The reality is that CLO equity is an illiquid asset that is best bought with the plan to hold to maturity because it can't be easily traded. That was equally true in June 2020, July 2021, and July 2025.

However, if we are comparing current NAVs to past NAVs, it is crucial that we take into account the change in price of the underlying assets. CLO equity is an investment in corporate leveraged loans. When you invest in CLO equity, you are essentially buying the last $10 of leveraged loans. The CLO equity absorbs the defaults, and in exchange gets materially higher cash flow than the debt tranches of a CLO. Naturally, the price of leveraged loans has a huge impact on the valuation of CLO equity. If leveraged loan prices are lower, then CLO equity is going to be lower.

The change in prices of leveraged loans is mostly a macro-driven factor. It is driven by a combination of interest rates, perceived credit risk, and investor demand for debt investments. We can expect CLO equity values to roughly follow changes in loan prices, albeit with a lot more volatility because CLO equity is a leveraged investment that amps up the risk/reward.

Here is what leveraged loans have done since June 2020:

They went up a lot in 2021, then crashed and ended down 1.99%. In other words, the loans that CLOs own are roughly the same price today as they were in June 2020. They are down a lot from 2021, and they are down slightly year-to-date.

ECC's NAV is roughly the same place it was in June 2020. It is down a lot from 2021. It is down slightly year-to-date.

The pattern is the same; the only difference is the magnitude. We expect CLO equity to be significantly more volatile than a loan ETF. Hopefully, by just looking at the chart, you can see how it is logical to compare a fund that holds leveraged investments in loans to a date when prices were similar, like 2020, but not very logical to compare it to 2021 when loan prices were materially different.

Through the cycle, ECC saw its NAV go up in 2021, it saw its NAV collapse in 2022, and it ended in about the same place, when loan prices also ended in about the same place. So no, ECC has not overpaid the past 5 years.

It is fair to note that this might not always be the case. From 2020 to 2025, we've seen loan prices change a lot, but we haven't seen a lot of defaults. Borrowers defaulting is where ECC is going to lose NAV permanently, if they are going to lose it. If loans suddenly went to 2021 prices tomorrow, ECC would go back to 2021 NAV. But that isn't likely to happen.

What's likely to happen is that we will probably see a wave of defaults and some kind of economic slowdown. ECC is probably going to realize some credit losses. That's just reality when you are lending; some borrowers won't pay you back, so don't lend to people you want to like.

So when loan prices go back to July 2021 levels, the calculation of ECC's NAV will be:

July 2021 NAV: realized credit losses + retained cash flow

That is assuming that loan prices and CLO equity prices are exactly the same as in July 2021. If they are different, you would add "+ Change in CLO prices" to the equation.

So, how much will ECC return going forward? Will we see another 5 years where ECC pays out $9.41 from their current $7.44+ in NAV? Nobody can know for sure because it is dependent upon unknown variables. Namely, realized losses and retained cash flow. To a lesser extent, issuing equity above NAV or buying back equity below NAV can help as well, but let's keep it simple.

ECC provides a convenient graphic in its earnings report showing the retained cash flow. Source

So over five quarters, ECC has had about $0.32 in cash flow exceeding its distributions and expenses.

What about credit losses? ECC has those, too. We can track them over time by adding up the realized gains/losses:

Over the past five quarters, these add up to a net loss of $0.32.

So over the past 5 quarters, ECC's portfolio has retained cash flow that has closely matched its realized losses. The bottom line is that for the past 5 quarters, its dividend was set perfectly, and all of the NAV change was driven by changing prices, not overpayments or credit losses.

What will happen going forward? Well, that's the thing about lending. You don't really know if the borrower will pay back until they do. There is certainly the possibility that defaults increase, and ECC will decide between lowering its distribution to match the decline in cash flow or overpaying and reducing NAV permanently.

We don't expect ECC's dividend to be fixed forever. That is an unrealistic expectation. What we expect is that ECC will pay out a ton of cash flow relative to NAV. Over the past 5 years, it has paid out $9.41 on $7.45 in NAV. Note that for an entire year, its distribution was 40% lower than it is now, and that's the result ECC achieved.

We expect ECC to provide hay while the sun is shining and to manage its portfolio and balance sheet responsibly when it isn't, so that when the sun comes back out as it did in 2021, it can see a meaningful recovery in NAV.

The last time loans were this cheap, ECC realized a return of 120% on NAV over 5 years. Will that feat be duplicated again? Maybe, maybe not. The future is uncertain until it happens. What I can say is that I love the cash flow I get from my CLO equity funds, and I'm willing to accept the gyrations in price and in dividends because I know loan prices will come and go, I know default waves will come and go, and I know that CLO equity has historically provided a materially higher total return than if I just invested in loans another way through BKLN or CLO debt tranches.

If you're looking for the kiddie rollercoaster, this isn't it. If you're looking for immense cash flow and you can keep your head when those about you are losing theirs, ECC might be for you. Especially when loans are trading at Black Swan prices when the current swan is grey at worst.

Conclusion

ECC is heavily beaten down by market sentiment. The last time we reached similar prices, we enjoyed massive returns in a short period. We expect we will enjoy a similar experience again.

Sentiment is an extremely mean mistress because she does not care about your feelings but is driven by the feelings of hundreds of thousands of faceless and nameless others who are engaged in the emotional psychology of short-term trading on the stock market. Thankfully, dividend investing and income investing work as a buffer against the insanity of others' emotions. You can't objectively determine how other people are going to feel about any situation, let alone how you are going to feel. But what you can't objectifiably determine is the type of income you want your portfolio to provide you, and use that income generation as a psychological safety blanket, protecting you from the buffeting blows of sentiment and emotional worry. Thereby, allowing you to be lathered in and bathing in a deep reservoir of wonderful recurring income. Just like you don't want to be emotionally driven in your retirement, don't allow your finances to be so.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Vietnam And Southeast Asia: Diversified Value And Growth
data.description:
Interest in Southeast Asia continues to increase amid shifting global supply chains, elevated geopolitical risks in China, and disinflationary trends. Within this context, Vietnam stands out as a leader in reform and industrial dynamism, while ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) offers breadth, scale, and macro resilience.

Vietnam: Structural Reforms with Trade Tailwinds

Trade Deals: Vietnam’s recent agreement with the U.S., imposing a 20% tariff on domestic exports (down from a proposed 46%) and removing tariffs on U.S. imports, offers clarity for investors. The deal includes a 40% levy on “transshipped” goods, underscoring U.S. efforts to curb Chinese circumvention via Vietnam.1 While details are pending, we think this marks a net-positive for long-term positioning and sets a potential template for other ASEAN trade negotiations.

Friendshoring: Vietnam continues to benefit from friendshoring trends, with foreign direct investment (FDI) rising 8.1% year over year in the first half of 2025.2 Manufacturing hubs have expanded across electronics, semiconductors, and garments, supported by bilateral trade clarity. U.S. firms are increasing supply chain exposure to Vietnam to mitigate concentration risk in China.

Demographics: With a median age of 32 and over 70% of the population in working age, Vietnam offers a dynamic labor force.3,4 Urbanization is accelerating, with a 3% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) in urban population growth fueling housing, infrastructure, and consumer demand while also historically leading to productivity gains.5

Structural Growth: The Vietnamese government is executing its most sweeping reforms since Doi Moi. Resolution 68 shifts the economic model toward private sector-led growth, streamlines bureaucracy through ministerial consolidation, and introduces R&D tax incentives (200% deduction).6 These changes are aimed at reducing the size of government, improving the ease of doing business, and supporting capital formation in industrials, banks, and consumer sectors.

Catalysts: Vietnam remains on the FTSE Russell watchlist for potential reclassification to secondary Emerging Market (EM) status. An upgrade could attract roughly $6 billion in index-driven flows.7

ASEAN: Long-Term Diversified Growth

Trade Deals: ASEAN countries have either reached a trade deal with the U.S. or have been subject to the baseline tariff rate, adding the policy certainty required for corporates to restart investment.

Friendshoring: ASEAN has become a magnet for supply chain relocation. Indonesia and Malaysia are expanding semiconductor capacity, while Singapore and Thailand are consolidating regional tech and logistics roles. FDI into the region surpassed $235 billion in 2024, an all-time high.8

Growth: ASEAN’s GDP is projected to grow 4.7% in 2025, driven by domestic consumption and infrastructure expansion.9 Indonesia’s interest rate cuts and Thailand’s $29 billion transport upgrade are examples of counter-cyclical fiscal support.

Valuations: After the region saw significant outflows post-U.S. election, ASEAN ex-Singapore/Indonesia is trading near historical troughs relative to broader EM.10 Multiples remain compressed, offering potential asymmetric upside if positioning normalizes.

Diversification: ASEAN economies are at varying stages of development and policy cycles. This heterogeneity offers natural diversification across growth drivers—energy in Malaysia, tourism in the Philippines, and logistics in Thailand.

Catalysts: Macroeconomic stabilization, progress in bilateral trade talks, and improving fund flows could lift investor sentiment. Structural reforms in individual markets—such as digitalization in Indonesia or tax policy shifts in Thailand—enhance long-term visibility.

Footnotes

1. CNN. (2025, Jul 2). Trump announces a US trade deal with Vietnam.

2. Trading Economics. Vietnam Foreign Direct Investment. Accessed July 10, 2025.

3. Geodatos. Population of Vietnam (2025 and historical). Accessed July 10, 2025.

4. Vinpearl. (2024, Jan 19). Living in Vietnam as a foreigner: A COMPLETE guide.

5. Global Data. (2025, April). The Urban Population of Vietnam (2021-2019, people).

6. Vietnam Investment Review. (2025, May 28). Resolution 68: Unlocking Vietnam's private sector.

7. Turicum Investment Management AG. Vietnam on Track for Emerging Market Recognition. Accessed July 10, 2025.

8. Wilson Center. (2025, April 1). 360° View of How Southeast Asia Can Attract More FDI in Chips and AI.

9. Reuters. (2025, April 9). US Tariffs, China Slowdown Cloud Developing Asia's Growth Outlook, Says ADB.

10. Bloomberg LP. FTSE/ASEAN 40 Index. Data as of July 10, 2025.

Glossary

Doi Moi: Economic and political reforms initiated in 1986 designed to move Vietnam from an isolate, centrally planned economy into a market economy open to international trade and investment.

Information provided by Global X Management Company LLC.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit nor guarantee against a loss.

The risks of foreign investments are typically greater in less developed countries, which are sometimes referred to as emerging markets. For example, legal, political, and economic structures in these countries may be changing rapidly, which can cause instability and greater risk of loss. These countries are also more likely to experience higher levels of inflation, deflation, or currency devaluation, which could hurt their economies and securities markets. For these and other reasons, investments in emerging markets are often considered speculative. Similarly, investors are also subject to foreign securities risks including, but not limited to, the fact that foreign investments may be subject to different and in some circumstances less stringent regulatory and disclosure standards than U.S. investments.
