data.title: Sinclair: Value Creation Time For Shareholders
data.description:
We were recently buyers of Sinclair, Inc. (NASDAQ:SBGI) shares on the sharp pullback after earnings, on the belief that we were purchasing shares at an extremely attractive valuation ahead of industry M&A. The timing of those purchases could not have been better for a trader, as news broke that Nexstar (NXST) and Tegna (TGNA) were in discussions to merge, which saw the entire sector rise, followed by news that Sinclair itself was beginning a comprehensive review of its business, which pushed shares higher the next day.

Sinclair issued a press release, which had the headline 'Sinclair Launches Comprehensive Strategic Review for Broadcast Business' but also stated that the Company was going to "simultaneously evaluate separation of Ventures". We believe that this is really good news for shareholders and could be the event many have been waiting for to unlock significant value.

The Backstory

Since the Diamond Sports RSN debacle, Sinclair has focused its assets into two "silos"—one with the legacy broadcast assets and the other housing investments that were related to the TV business, as well as private equity and real estate plays. Management has often discussed how the "ventures" segment had a lot of hidden value and that they were now focusing on this area of the company's holdings to help unlock value for shareholders. While effort has been put into the Tennis Channel (we would note that the Company did lose the French Open rights) to modernize it and offer it as a DTC product, the run-off of private equity investments has generated cash and allowed management to build a sizeable cash reserve ($393 million as of Q2 2025) to make future investments with but also figure out the path forward for the Company as a whole.

During the quarterly conference call, the management team again stressed that the cash and investment portfolio at Ventures had a book value at quarter's end of $726.4 million, of which $392.6 million was cash. With 45,871,818 Class A shares and 23,775,056 Class B shares outstanding, the book value on Ventures alone is $10.42/share, with over half of that in easy-to-access liquid cash/cash equivalents. We would note that if the book value of The Tennis Channel and other operating assets controlled by Ventures were included, the book value would surpass Sinclair's current market cap. However, we are not sure that the value of The Tennis Channel has held up since the Company acquired it nearly 10 years ago.

While we do think that there is some upside from the book values listed, we would note that management did indicate that the Bally's book value was mark-to-market and thus, when combined with cash, shows the current value of over $500 million of Venture's $726.4 million investment portfolio. In short, the gains that we suspect exist for the Real Estate, Direct Investments, and Private Equity/Venture Capital line items are probably significant on a percentage basis for those categories, but for the entire portfolio, it certainly appears that investors have decent estimates for valuations.

The Path Forward

We see three distinct possibilities that Sinclair could choose to pursue moving forward:

  1. Split the Company into two entities, one focusing on broadcast television and the other on venture investments.
  2. Keep the Company together, but utilize the current cash from Ventures to do M&A for broadcast television.
  3. Sell the broadcast business off—either in whole or piecemeal—to the highest bidders.

While the first option would fit the traditional activist investor playbook, we believe that it would create too much confusion in the market and be a major distraction for the Company during what could be one of the last M&A cycles in the industry. Because of this, we think the Company needs to pass on the first option to take a more pragmatic approach to the current M&A cycle within the broadcast industry. (While we understand the recent press release seemed to indicate that this was the likely path, we do not see the scale on that side of the business to justify a split.)

With Nexstar and Tegna agreeing to merge, it leaves a handful of entities with controlling shareholders looking for merger partners—yet the problem is that most have controlling shareholders that appear focused on continuing to be involved. Gray Media (GTN) and EW Scripps (SSP) find themselves in the same position as Sinclair, and we know that Gray views themselves as a buyer, not a seller. If Sinclair now views themselves as a buyer and not a seller too, then we believe that Sinclair will need to utilize the cash from the Ventures side of the company to engage in M&A—either alone or teaming up with another partner—to find a target to buy and split the assets in the most beneficial way to optimize the deal's economics for the buyers.

Depending on the target and depending on the deal structure, we can envision a few scenarios for Sinclair where they could pay the seller a premium multiple but also create economics that allow the deal to be both accretive and deleverage the balance sheet in the near term. If, for instance, Gray and Sinclair were able to convince Scripps to do a deal, the two buyers would have some firepower to work with as their average monthly per-subscriber retransmission fee works out to roughly 30% above Scripps. These rates get adjusted upward after an acquisition to the acquirer's rate, and it is this deal accretion that we see providing the most flexibility for Sinclair to keep leverage in check while still offering attractive terms to interested sellers.

Never Say Never

While Sinclair certainly appears to be one of the companies able to consolidate the industry (and not likely to be sellers, or at least net sellers), the possibility does exist that the Smith family decides that now is the time to sell and looks for a deal that sees them give up control but maintain a sizeable stake while also deleveraging the newly created entity. This could be done by Sinclair selling to Gray with a third party involved (PE-backed, most likely) to take control of stations that overlap, have less appealing economics, or pose antitrust issues by kicking cash into the deal. Accepting a mix of cash and stock helps with the tax hit and putting cash into shareholders' pockets, but it also would allow the newly formed entity to either maintain or improve leverage ratios, as retransmission fee accretion would be minimal, and deal economics would be driven mostly by cost-cutting and other efficiencies. With the Smiths taking a mix of stock and cash, it also allows Gray's controlling family to maintain control while also using the Ventures' cash to pay down the Sinclair debt they will inherit and refinance.

M&A Just Getting Started

Regardless of what has already happened in the industry, we believe that the next year or two will see a lot of additional transactions in this space. There are private equity funds eager to buy in, other funds eager to finance deals, and numerous station groups analyzing what deals make the most sense for them moving forward. While we believe that one of the three family-controlled broadcasters will sell during this window, it is quite possible that all three remain independent and move forward by sourcing smaller deals; rather than a transformative acquisition, they will each have to grind it out wheeling and dealing with other operators.

Final Thoughts

We generally dislike sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) discussions on assets that are carried on the books at historical prices, and management makes arguments about "hidden value". At Sinclair, there is a lot less guessing as to the accuracy of the argument, as a large portion of the Ventures investment portfolio now sits in cash and cash equivalents, with the Bally's investment being marked-to-market. While it is entirely possible that The Tennis Channel and its new DTC properties have seen their value fall since being purchased a decade ago, if we look at the fact that there is roughly $5.64 per share in cash within the Ventures segment, we think that alone provides plenty of upside (potentially 50%) for the shares if deployed properly for M&A activities.

If Sinclair struggles to find a dance partner and is forced to "go it alone", we think a mix of share buybacks and select debt repurchases should be utilized. While some may argue that the Company should focus exclusively on debt repurchases in that scenario, we would note that the cash should currently generate enough in either interest income or earnings credits to offset roughly half of the debt expense (Sinclair has two bonds of note with coupons of 8.125% and 9.75%, and we think they can generate roughly 3.75-4.25% at the bank via earnings credits or interest income via short-term cash instruments with their cash). So share repurchases at current levels are about as attractive as debt repurchases and most likely have a greater impact on helping increase the company's share price.

We remain buyers of Sinclair and the industry as a whole, as we believe the current M&A window will help the entire industry work out its leverage/balance sheet issues and cut out duplicative expenses both at the corporate level as well as the local level, where two stations can be owned.

We prefer to buy shares below the $15.50/share level, but for long-term buyers, value all the way up to $17.50/share.

data.title: AMD: This Is Your Exit Opportunity
data.description:
Earlier this month, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) reported decent Q2 results, which showed modest revenue growth and strong profitability. However, the latest developments within the semiconductor industry suggest the market is overly optimistic about the company’s ability to continue to aggressively grow in the near term. There are already signs that the data center sales are slowing, the gaming business is underperforming, and the AI server capabilities may not scale quickly enough to offset cyclical headwinds in other segments. At the same time, rising geopolitical risks could also undermine future growth. Because of that, we continue to remain bearish about AMD and stick with our Sell rating for the company.

The Market Has Become Overly Excited

To this day, AMD’s data center segment remains the company’s key driver of growth, and in Q2, it generated $3.2 billion in revenues, up 14% Y/Y. While the segment continues to grow at a double-digit rate, the growth rate itself has slowed down in comparison to the 80% Y/Y growth rate that was achieved in the same quarter last year. Although the company’s Instinct MI300 accelerators continue to gain traction, the management acknowledged that supply constraints and competitive pricing from Nvidia (NVDA) and Intel (INTC) are likely to limit near-term growth. Also, the cloud vendors are showing signs of more selective spending, with some prioritizing AI-enabled GPUs over general-purpose processors. That shift is putting pressure on AMD’s EPYC server processors, which are facing a more competitive environment.

In addition to that, AMD remains exposed to geopolitical risks, particularly in China, which is a key market for both its data center and client segments. The American export restrictions on advanced AI chips have already impacted AMD’s ability to sell its high-performance accelerators to Chinese customers. Although the management has said that it is working on compatible versions of the MI300 chips, the history shows that restricted SKUs often have significantly lower margins and limited demand. Also, the U.S. Department of Commerce is now tightening controls on semiconductor exports to several countries, creating uncertainty for future revenue streams across the globe. While AMD’s diversification into other, more reliable regions is helping the company offset some of the downside from restrictions, replacing high-value sales from China will likely be challenging and could weigh on profitability over the next few quarters.

Finally, AMD’s aggressive push into AI and specialty silicon is driving higher capital spending. In Q2, the spending on research and development reached $2 billion, or 21 percent of revenue, which reflects significant investments in new architectures and technology nodes. While R&D spending is important to remain competitive, it also has a negative impact on cash flow generation in the near term. In Q2, AMD generated only $1.1 billion in free cash flow compared to $1.5 billion last year, despite higher overall revenue. Also, the upcoming releases of Zen 5 processors and MI400 GPUs will require additional marketing and manufacturing commitments, further constraining cash flow.

With gross margin growth not expected to be significant in the near term, AMD may face difficulty balancing shareholder returns with the need to fund innovation at current levels. Because of that, we believe that the market has become overly excited about AMD’s latest growth and is ignoring the major risks that the company now faces.

Risk To Our Bearish Thesis

Several factors could undermine our bearish views. Faster-than-expected growth in MI300 shipments, combined with strong adoption of Zen 5-based EPYC processors, could accelerate growth in the data center market in the upcoming quarters. The gaming sector could also rebound if console refresh cycles accelerate, and consumer demand recovers in key markets.

In addition, easing export restrictions on more advanced chips or favorable geopolitical events could restore access to the high margin Chinese market. However, even under these optimistic scenarios, AMD will still face strong competitive pressure from Nvidia in AI accelerators and Intel in server processors, making it difficult to continue to expand margins.

Another factor that could challenge our pessimistic outlook is AMD’s diversified product portfolio. The company operates in the desktop, mobile, and could markets, which protects it from macroeconomic volatility. This broad range of activities could allow AMD to offset weakness in one segment with strength in another, leading to better-than-expected results in the following quarters.

The Real Value of AMD

Our valuation model, which is available below, was built when AMD’s stock was trading at $176.14 per share. In the model, we applied a 3 percent perpetual growth rate, consistent with the assumptions we typically use for comparable semiconductor peers. The forecast spans the next five years and incorporates inputs such as cash and debt, which have been taken from the most recent earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 10.82%. The discount rate was determined by combining AMD’s cost of equity and cost of debt. Debt costs were based on TTM results, while equity costs were calculated using the CAPM method, where the risk-free rate was 4.32%, a beta of 1.92, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

In our forecast, sales and EBIT estimates are broadly aligned with market consensus over the next five years. The model assumes a 15 percent tax rate, consistent with the level used in our previous valuation models for other semiconductor companies. The changes in net working capital in our model are expected to be 1% of sales, which is the rate that we also used when creating a valuation model for other semiconductor businesses. All other inputs are kept consistent with AMD’s historical performance.

Based on these assumptions, our model values AMD’s enterprise value at $101.5 billion. After adding cash and cash equivalents and subtracting total debt, we arrived at an equity value of $102 billion. On a per-share basis, this implies an intrinsic value of $62.91 per share, which represents a discount of about 64.3% to the current market price.

Final Thoughts

AMD remains one of the leading companies in high-performance computing, but its growth profile is slowing, and significant challenges lie ahead. Data center momentum is slowing, regulatory risks are rising, and capital expenditures are weighing on free cash flow. The current valuation leaves little room for execution error. For these reasons, we believe AMD’s risk-to-reward profile is unfavorable at current levels, and that is why the company remains a Sell for us.
